title: Preparing thermal states of frustrated quantum spin systems using 139 qubits
authors: Roland C. Farrell, Yongtao Zhan, Lucas Katschke, Lode Pollet, Ilan T. Rosen, Jad C. Halimeh
abstract: Finite-temperature properties of strongly correlated quantum matter are central to condensed matter, chemistry, and high-energy physics, yet are often inaccessible to classical methods such as quantum Monte Carlo (QMC). Here, we investigate dissipative thermal state preparation of frustrated spin systems using digital quantum computers. We focus on two paradigmatic models on the kagome lattice: the antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model (AFHM), whose finite-temperature properties are inaccessible to QMC due to a severe sign problem, and the antiferromagnetic Ising model (AFIM), which serves as a sign-problem-free benchmark. Using IBM quantum processors, we prepare approximate thermal states of the AFIM on kagome lattices with up to $79$ spins coupled to $60$ environment qubits. We observe the emergence of a robust steady state with an adjustable effective temperature that persists in circuits with over 1000 layers of two-qubit gates. We further study the scalability of the dissipative protocol through classical statevector simulations of the AFIM and AFHM. On lattices with up to 24 sites, we find that the circuit depth to reach thermal equilibrium is independent of system size and grows at most linearly with inverse temperature. These results establish engineered dissipation as a promising approach to finite-temperature quantum simulation of frustrated matter, and point toward regimes where quantum devices may outperform classical methods.
summary: The researchers developed a method to prepare thermal states of frustrated kagome spin systems using dissipative quantum computing on IBM hardware. They found that the time needed to reach equilibrium does not depend on the number of spins, suggesting a potential quantum advantage over classical methods. The study also quantifies how hardware noise and reset errors affect the accuracy of the prepared thermal states.
main_problem: The difficulty of preparing thermal (Gibbs) states of frustrated quantum spin systems on digital quantum computers due to the sign problem in classical Monte Carlo methods and the impact of hardware noise.
main_result: Demonstrated a robust dissipative protocol on IBM quantum processors that prepares approximate thermal states of the kagome Ising model, showing that the required circuit depth scales at most linearly with inverse temperature and is independent of system size.
method: A dissipative quantum Gibbs sampling protocol using engineered environment qubits, mid-circuit resets, and a quantum channel designed to satisfy approximate quantum detailed balance, supplemented by Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE) for error mitigation.
model_or_system: Frustrated spin systems on a kagome lattice, specifically the Antiferromagnetic Heisenberg Model (AFHM) and the Antiferromagnetic Ising Model (AFIM), implemented on IBM's heavy-hex superconducting quantum processors.
key_observables: Energy density, local magnetization, antiferromagnetic correlations, and triangle ZZ correlators.
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), system size (Ns), number of environment qubits (Ne), and coupling strength (alpha).
main_assumptions: The efficiency of the protocol assumes that the mixing time scaling observed in small-scale classical simulations persists to larger system sizes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the dissipative algorithm and reset process; Figure 2 shows the low-energy spectrum, mixing time scaling, and convergence analysis; Figure 3 shows the kagome lattice mapping onto IBM hardware and circuit diagrams; Figure 4 compares experimental energy density to noiseless and QMC results; Figure 13 shows reset error confusion matrices and spatial maps of effective temperature; Figure 14 shows energy density evolution with error mitigation; Figure 20 displays spatially resolved magnetization and correlators.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermal state preparation, describes the dissipative sampling protocol and its implementation on IBM hardware, presents scaling and convergence results from classical simulations, details the experimental implementation and error mitigation strategies, and concludes with a comparison of experimental observables against QMC benchmarks.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it demonstrates a practical method for using engineered dissipation and mid-circuit measurements to drive a system toward a target many-body steady state.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a The thermal state preparation algorithm from Ref. [22]. Qubits are split into system (S) and environment (E). The system qubits are acted on by the quantum channel Φ a total of Nresets times. This produces an ensemble of pure states {|ψout⟩} that are sampled from ˜ρS(β) which approximates the Gibbs ensemble of the system Hamiltonian HS at inverse temperature β. b The quantum channel Φ begins by initializing each environment qubit to a random product state sampled from the Gibbs ensemble of the environment Hamiltonian HE. Next, evolution for time T is implemented in discrete time steps δt. The total Hamiltonian contains HS, HE and a system-environment interaction component, HSE,...

FIG. 2. a The low energy spectrum of the AFIM for gz = 2, gx = 0.5. b The mixing time, τmix(Fthresh) for NS = 12, a range of β, and in the AFIM (left) and AFHM (right). The inset gives the mixing time for a larger fidelity threshold in the AFIM showing a local maxima at the ice crossover, βc ≈1.4. The bottom plots show the β-dependence of the average magnetization MZ in the AFIM and the square of the total spin Stot · Stot in the AFHM. c The convergence of the quantum Gibbs samplers for increasing lattice sizes. The upper plots show the fidelity in the AFIM with β = 3 (left) and AFHM with β = 4 (right). The bottom panels show the ratio of the measured energy E to the exact energy Eexact....

FIG. 3. a Embedding of a kagome lattice (left) onto the heavy-hex connectivity of IBM’s Heron r3 processors (right). The heavy-hex connectivity is represented by the gray links. The sites (links) of the kagome lattice are defined by the white colored qubits (pink links). The environment qubits (black colored qubits) are interspersed throughout the lattice. b The quantum circuits used in one application of the dissipative quantum channel Φ for (NS, NE) = (12, 12) on ibm boston. The circuits highlighted in purple correspond to one second order Trotter step, which is repeated T/δt = 3 times. After time evolution, the environment qubits are measured and reset to |0⟩. c The definition of the...

The data for NS = 12, 18 is compared to noiseless statevector simulations with environment temperature β (dashed). The x-axis is rescaled to compare the results across different NE/NS as explained in Appendix A. The spatial distribution of magnetization c and △ZZ d measured in the approximate thermal states for NS = 79. The spins in c that are outlined in red surround a hot environment qubit with a large reset error rate. FIG. 5. Matrix elements of the jump operators O be- tween the 50 lowest-lying energy eigenstates of the AFIM and AFHM on a NS = 18 site kagome lattice with PBCs. The opacity of the pixel at (n, m) represents max (|⟨Em|O|En⟩|) where the maximum is taken over all jump operators. The pixels are orange if the maximum is a single site jump op- erator and teal if it is a SU(2) singlet jump operator. The opacity has been normalized by the largest entry for each of the two models independently. The bottom panels show the energy of the corresponding eigenstates.
